    3251              : /*
    3252              :  * preprocess_groupclause - do preparatory work on GROUP BY clause
    3253              :  *
    3254              :  * The idea here is to adjust the ordering of the GROUP BY elements
    3255              :  * (which in itself is semantically insignificant) to match ORDER BY,
    3256              :  * thereby allowing a single sort operation to both implement the ORDER BY
    3257              :  * requirement and set up for a Unique step that implements GROUP BY.
    3258              :  *
    3259              :  * In principle it might be interesting to consider other orderings of the
    3260              :  * GROUP BY elements, which could match the sort ordering of other
    3261              :  * possible plans (eg an indexscan) and thereby reduce cost.  We don't
    3262              :  * bother with that, though.  Hashed grouping will frequently win anyway.
    3263              :  *
    3264              :  * Note: we need no comparable processing of the distinctClause because
    3265              :  * the parser already enforced that that matches ORDER BY.
    3266              :  *
    3267              :  * For grouping sets, the order of items is instead forced to agree with that
    3268              :  * of the grouping set (and items not in the grouping set are skipped). The
    3269              :  * work of sorting the order of grouping set elements to match the ORDER BY if
    3270              :  * possible is done elsewhere.
    3271              :  */
    3272              : static List *
    3273           25 : preprocess_groupclause(PlannerInfo *root, List *force)
    3274              : {
    3275           25 :         Query      *parse = root->parse;
    3276           25 :         List       *new_groupclause = NIL;
    3277              :         bool            partial_match;
    3278              :         ListCell   *sl;
    3279              :         ListCell   *gl;
    3280              : 
    3281              :         /* For grouping sets, we need to force the ordering */
    3282           25 :         if (force)
    3283              :         {
    3284           44 :                 foreach(sl, force)
    3285              :                 {
    3286           22 :                         Index           ref = lfirst_int(sl);
    3287           22 :                         SortGroupClause *cl = get_sortgroupref_clause(ref, parse->groupClause);
    3288              : 
    3289           22 :                         new_groupclause = lappend(new_groupclause, cl);
    3290              :                 }
    3291              : 
    3292           22 :                 return new_groupclause;
    3293              :         }
    3294              : 
    3295              :         /* If no ORDER BY, nothing useful to do here */
    3296            3 :         if (parse->sortClause == NIL)
    3297            3 :                 return parse->groupClause;
    3298              : 
    3299              :         /*
    3300              :          * Scan the ORDER BY clause and construct a list of matching GROUP BY
    3301              :          * items, but only as far as we can make a matching prefix.
    3302              :          *
    3303              :          * This code assumes that the sortClause contains no duplicate items.
    3304              :          */
    3305            0 :         foreach(sl, parse->sortClause)
    3306              :         {
    3307            0 :                 SortGroupClause *sc = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, sl);
    3308              : 
    3309            0 :                 foreach(gl, parse->groupClause)
    3310              :                 {
    3311            0 :                         SortGroupClause *gc = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, gl);
    3312              : 
    3313            0 :                         if (equal(gc, sc))
    3314              :                         {
    3315            0 :                                 new_groupclause = lappend(new_groupclause, gc);
    3316            0 :                                 break;
    3317              :                         }
    3318              :                 }
    3319            0 :                 if (gl == NULL)
    3320            0 :                         break;                          /* no match, so stop scanning */
    3321              :         }
    3322              : 
    3323              :         /* Did we match all of the ORDER BY list, or just some of it? */
    3324            0 :         partial_match = (sl != NULL);
    3325              : 
    3326              :         /* If no match at all, no point in reordering GROUP BY */
    3327            0 :         if (new_groupclause == NIL)
    3328            0 :                 return parse->groupClause;
    3329              : 
    3330              :         /*
    3331              :          * Add any remaining GROUP BY items to the new list, but only if we were
    3332              :          * able to make a complete match.  In other words, we only rearrange the
    3333              :          * GROUP BY list if the result is that one list is a prefix of the other
    3334              :          * --- otherwise there's no possibility of a common sort.  Also, give up
    3335              :          * if there are any non-sortable GROUP BY items, since then there's no
    3336              :          * hope anyway.
    3337              :          */
    3338            0 :         foreach(gl, parse->groupClause)
    3339              :         {
    3340            0 :                 SortGroupClause *gc = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, gl);
    3341              : 
    3342            0 :                 if (list_member_ptr(new_groupclause, gc))
    3343            0 :                         continue;                       /* it matched an ORDER BY item */
    3344            0 :                 if (partial_match)
    3345            0 :                         return parse->groupClause;   /* give up, no common sort possible */
    3346            0 :                 if (!OidIsValid(gc->sortop))
    3347            0 :                         return parse->groupClause;   /* give up, GROUP BY can't be sorted */
    3348            0 :                 new_groupclause = lappend(new_groupclause, gc);
    3349              :         }
    3350              : 
    3351              :         /* Success --- install the rearranged GROUP BY list */
    3352              :         Assert(list_length(parse->groupClause) == list_length(new_groupclause));
    3353            0 :         return new_groupclause;
    3354              : }
    3355              : 
    3356              : /*
    3357              :  * Extract lists of grouping sets that can be implemented using a single
    3358              :  * rollup-type aggregate pass each. Returns a list of lists of grouping sets.
    3359              :  *
    3360              :  * Input must be sorted with smallest sets first. Result has each sublist
    3361              :  * sorted with smallest sets first.
    3362              :  *
    3363              :  * We want to produce the absolute minimum possible number of lists here to
    3364              :  * avoid excess sorts. Fortunately, there is an algorithm for this; the problem
    3365              :  * of finding the minimal partition of a partially-ordered set into chains
    3366              :  * (which is what we need, taking the list of grouping sets as a poset ordered
    3367              :  * by set inclusion) can be mapped to the problem of finding the maximum
    3368              :  * cardinality matching on a bipartite graph, which is solvable in polynomial
    3369              :  * time with a worst case of no worse than O(n^2.5) and usually much
    3370              :  * better. Since our N is at most 4096, we don't need to consider fallbacks to
    3371              :  * heuristic or approximate methods.  (Planning time for a 12-d cube is under

    3424              :          *

    3435            5 :         j = 0;
    3436            5 :         i = 1;

    3445           18 :                 foreach(lc2, candidate)

    3448              :                 }
    3449              : 
    3450              :                 /* we can only be a dup if we're the same length as a previous set */

    3452              :                 {
    3453              :                         int                     k;
    3454              : 
    3455            8 :                         for (k = j; k < i; ++k)
    3456              :                         {

    3458              :                                 {

    3500            9 :                         ++i;

    3506              :         /*

    3574              : /*
    3575              :  * Reorder the elements of a list of grouping sets such that they have correct
    3576              :  * prefix relationships. Also inserts the GroupingSetData annotations.
    3577              :  *
    3578              :  * The input must be ordered with smallest sets first; the result is returned
    3579              :  * with largest sets first.  Note that the result shares no list substructure
    3580              :  * with the input, so it's safe for the caller to modify it later.
    3581              :  *
    3582              :  * If we're passed in a sortclause, we follow its order of columns to the
    3583              :  * extent possible, to minimize the chance that we add unnecessary sorts.
    3584              :  * (We're trying here to ensure that GROUPING SETS ((a,b,c),(c)) ORDER BY c,b,a
    3585              :  * gets implemented in one pass.)
    3586              :  */
    3587              : static List *
    3588           11 : reorder_grouping_sets(List *groupingsets, List *sortclause)
    3589              : {
    3590              :         ListCell   *lc;
    3591           11 :         List       *previous = NIL;
    3592           11 :         List       *result = NIL;
    3593              : 
    3594           27 :         foreach(lc, groupingsets)
    3595              :         {
    3596           16 :                 List       *candidate = (List *) lfirst(lc);
    3597           16 :                 List       *new_elems = list_difference_int(candidate, previous);
    3598           16 :                 GroupingSetData *gs = makeNode(GroupingSetData);
    3599              : 
    3600           16 :                 while (list_length(sortclause) > list_length(previous) &&
    3601            0 :                            list_length(new_elems) > 0)
    3602              :                 {
    3603            0 :                         SortGroupClause *sc = list_nth(sortclause, list_length(previous));
    3604            0 :                         int                     ref = sc->tleSortGroupRef;
    3605              : 
    3606            0 :                         if (list_member_int(new_elems, ref))
    3607              :                         {
    3608            0 :                                 previous = lappend_int(previous, ref);
    3609            0 :                                 new_elems = list_delete_int(new_elems, ref);
    3610              :                         }
    3611              :                         else
    3612              :                         {
    3613              :                                 /* diverged from the sortclause; give up on it */
    3614            0 :                                 sortclause = NIL;
    3615            0 :                                 break;
    3616              :                         }
    3617              :                 }
    3618              : 
    3619           16 :                 previous = list_concat(previous, new_elems);
    3620              : 
    3621           16 :                 gs->set = list_copy(previous);
    3622           16 :                 result = lcons(gs, result);
    3623              :         }
    3624              : 
    3625           11 :         list_free(previous);
    3626              : 
    3627           11 :         return result;
    3628              : }

    3630              : /*
    3631              :  * Compute query_pathkeys and other pathkeys during plan generation

    3659              :

    3665          486 :                 root->window_pathkeys = NIL;
    3666              : 
    3667          486 :         if (parse->distinctClause &&
    3668            0 :                 grouping_is_sortable(parse->distinctClause))
    3669            0 :                 root->distinct_pathkeys =
    3670            0 :                         make_pathkeys_for_sortclauses(root,
    3671              :                                                                                   parse->distinctClause,
    3672              :                                                                                   tlist);
    3673              :         else
    3674          486 :                 root->distinct_pathkeys = NIL;

    3681              :         /*
    3682              :          * Figure out whether we want a sorted result from query_planner.

    3685              :          * properly for grouping.  Otherwise, if we have window functions to
    3686              :          * evaluate, we try to sort for the first window.  Otherwise, if there's a
    3687              :          * sortable DISTINCT clause that's more rigorous than the ORDER BY clause,
    3688              :          * we try to produce output that's sufficiently well sorted for the
    3689              :          * DISTINCT.  Otherwise, if there is an ORDER BY clause, we want to sort
    3690              :          * by the ORDER BY clause.
    3691              :          *
    3692              :          * Note: if we have both ORDER BY and GROUP BY, and ORDER BY is a superset
    3693              :          * of GROUP BY, it would be tempting to request sort by ORDER BY --- but
    3694              :          * that might just leave us failing to exploit an available sort order at
    3695              :          * all.  Needs more thought.  The choice for DISTINCT versus ORDER BY is
    3696              :          * much easier, since we know that the parser ensured that one is a
    3697              :          * superset of the other.

    3700            8 :                 root->query_pathkeys = root->group_pathkeys;
    3701          478 :         else if (root->window_pathkeys)
    3702            0 :                 root->query_pathkeys = root->window_pathkeys;
    3703          956 :         else if (list_length(root->distinct_pathkeys) >
    3704          478 :                          list_length(root->sort_pathkeys))
    3705            0 :                 root->query_pathkeys = root->distinct_pathkeys;

    3709          465 :                 root->query_pathkeys = NIL;

    3712              : /*
    3713              :  * Estimate number of groups produced by grouping clauses (1 if not grouping)

    3825              : 
    3826              : /*
    3827              :  * create_grouping_paths
    3828              :  *
    3829              :  * Build a new upperrel containing Paths for grouping and/or aggregation.
    3830              :  * Along the way, we also build an upperrel for Paths which are partially
    3831              :  * grouped and/or aggregated.  A partially grouped and/or aggregated path
    3832              :  * needs a FinalizeAggregate node to complete the aggregation.  Currently,
    3833              :  * the only partially grouped paths we build are also partial paths; that
    3834              :  * is, they need a Gather and then a FinalizeAggregate.
    3835              :  *
    3836              :  * input_rel: contains the source-data Paths
    3837              :  * target: the pathtarget for the result Paths to compute
    3838              :  * agg_costs: cost info about all aggregates in query (in AGGSPLIT_SIMPLE mode)
    3839              :  * gd: grouping sets data including list of grouping sets and their clauses
    3840              :  *
    3841              :  * Note: all Paths in input_rel are expected to return the target computed
    3842              :  * by make_group_input_target.
    3843              :  */
    3844              : static RelOptInfo *
    3845           17 : create_grouping_paths(PlannerInfo *root,
    3846              :                                           RelOptInfo *input_rel,
    3847              :                                           PathTarget *target,
    3848              :                                           bool target_parallel_safe,
    3849              :                                           const AggClauseCosts *agg_costs,
    3850              :                                           grouping_sets_data *gd)
    3851              : {
    3852           17 :         Query      *parse = root->parse;
    3853              :         RelOptInfo *grouped_rel;
    3854              :         RelOptInfo *partially_grouped_rel;
    3855              : 
    3856              :         /*
    3857              :          * Create grouping relation to hold fully aggregated grouping and/or
    3858              :          * aggregation paths.
    3859              :          */
    3860           17 :         grouped_rel = make_grouping_rel(root, input_rel, target,
    3861              :                                                                         target_parallel_safe, parse->havingQual);
    3862              : 
    3863              :         /*
    3864              :          * Create either paths for a degenerate grouping or paths for ordinary
    3865              :          * grouping, as appropriate.
    3866              :          */
    3867           17 :         if (is_degenerate_grouping(root))
    3868            0 :                 create_degenerate_grouping_paths(root, input_rel, grouped_rel);
    3869              :         else
    3870              :         {
    3871           17 :                 int                     flags = 0;
    3872              :                 GroupPathExtraData extra;
    3873              : 
    3874              :                 /*
    3875              :                  * Determine whether it's possible to perform sort-based
    3876              :                  * implementations of grouping.  (Note that if groupClause is empty,
    3877              :                  * grouping_is_sortable() is trivially true, and all the

    4435            7 :                 if (availspace > 0 && list_length(gd->rollups) > 1)

    4439              :                         int                     k_capacity;

    4441            6 :                         Bitmapset  *hash_items = NULL;
    4442              :                         int                     i;
    4443              : 
    4444              :                         /*
    4445              :                          * We treat this as a knapsack problem: the knapsack capacity
    4446              :                          * represents hash_mem, the item weights are the estimated memory
    4447              :                          * usage of the hashtables needed to implement a single rollup,
    4448              :                          * and we really ought to use the cost saving as the item value;
    4449              :                          * however, currently the costs assigned to sort nodes don't
    4450              :                          * reflect the comparison costs well, and so we treat all items as
    4451              :                          * of equal value (each rollup we hash instead saves us one sort).
    4452              :                          *
    4453              :                          * To use the discrete knapsack, we need to scale the values to a
    4454              :                          * reasonably small bounded range.  We choose to allow a 5% error
    4455              :                          * margin; we have no more than 4096 rollups in the worst possible
    4456              :                          * case, which with a 5% error margin will require a bit over 42MB
    4457              :                          * of workspace. (Anyone wanting to plan queries that complex had
    4458              :                          * better have the memory for it.  In more reasonable cases, with
    4459              :                          * no more than a couple of dozen rollups, the memory usage will
    4460              :                          * be negligible.)
    4461              :                          *
    4462              :                          * k_capacity is naturally bounded, but we clamp the values for
    4463              :                          * scale and weight (below) to avoid overflows or underflows (or
    4464              :                          * uselessly trying to use a scale factor less than 1 byte).
    4465              :                          */
    4466            6 :                         scale = Max(availspace / (20.0 * num_rollups), 1.0);
    4467            6 :                         k_capacity = (int) floor(availspace / scale);
    4468              : 
    4469              :                         /*
    4470              :                          * We leave the first rollup out of consideration since it's the

    4479              : 
    4480            6 :                                 if (rollup->hashable)
    4481              :                                 {
    4482            6 :                                         double          sz = estimate_hashagg_tablesize(path,

    4493              :                                 }

    4495              : 
    4496              :                         /*

    4505            6 :                         if (!bms_is_empty(hash_items))

    4507            6 :                                 rollups = list_make1(linitial(gd->rollups));

    4509            6 :                                 i = 0;
    4510           12 :                                 for_each_from(lc, gd->rollups, 1)
    4511              :                                 {

    4514            6 :                                         if (rollup->hashable)

    4518            6 :                                                                                                         rollup->gsets_data);
    4519              :                                                 else
    4520            0 :                                                         rollups = lappend(rollups, rollup);
    4521            6 :                                                 ++i;
    4522              :                                         }
    4523              :                                         else
    4524            0 :                                                 rollups = lappend(rollups, rollup);

    4526              :                         }
    4527              :                 }
    4528              : 
    4529            7 :                 if (!rollups && hash_sets)

    4665              : /*

    4698              :          * contain all window partitioning and sorting expressions, to ensure

    4704              : 
    4705            0 :         foreach(l, activeWindows)

    5215              : 
    5216              : /*
    5217              :  * make_group_input_target
    5218              :  *        Generate appropriate PathTarget for initial input to grouping nodes.
    5219              :  *
    5220              :  * If there is grouping or aggregation, the scan/join subplan cannot emit
    5221              :  * the query's final targetlist; for example, it certainly can't emit any
    5222              :  * aggregate function calls.  This routine generates the correct target
    5223              :  * for the scan/join subplan.
    5224              :  *
    5225              :  * The query target list passed from the parser already contains entries
    5226              :  * for all ORDER BY and GROUP BY expressions, but it will not have entries
    5227              :  * for variables used only in HAVING clauses; so we need to add those
    5228              :  * variables to the subplan target list.  Also, we flatten all expressions
    5229              :  * except GROUP BY items into their component variables; other expressions
    5230              :  * will be computed by the upper plan nodes rather than by the subplan.
    5231              :  * For example, given a query like
    5232              :  *              SELECT a+b,SUM(c+d) FROM table GROUP BY a+b;
    5233              :  * we want to pass this targetlist to the subplan:
    5234              :  *              a+b,c,d
    5235              :  * where the a+b target will be used by the Sort/Group steps, and the
    5236              :  * other targets will be used for computing the final results.
    5237              :  *
    5238              :  * 'final_target' is the query's final target list (in PathTarget form)
    5239              :  *
    5240              :  * The result is the PathTarget to be computed by the Paths returned from
    5241              :  * query_planner().
    5242              :  */
    5243              : static PathTarget *
    5244           17 : make_group_input_target(PlannerInfo *root, PathTarget *final_target)
    5245              : {
    5246           17 :         Query      *parse = root->parse;
    5247              :         PathTarget *input_target;
    5248              :         List       *non_group_cols;
    5249              :         List       *non_group_vars;
    5250              :         int                     i;
    5251              :         ListCell   *lc;
    5252              : 
    5253              :         /*
    5254              :          * We must build a target containing all grouping columns, plus any other
    5255              :          * Vars mentioned in the query's targetlist and HAVING qual.
    5256              :          */
    5257           17 :         input_target = create_empty_pathtarget();
    5258           17 :         non_group_cols = NIL;
    5259              : 
    5260           17 :         i = 0;
    5261           55 :         foreach(lc, final_target->exprs)
    5262              :         {
    5263           38 :                 Expr       *expr = (Expr *) lfirst(lc);
    5264           38 :                 Index           sgref = get_pathtarget_sortgroupref(final_target, i);
    5265              : 
    5266           50 :                 if (sgref && parse->groupClause &&
    5267           12 :                         get_sortgroupref_clause_noerr(sgref, parse->groupClause) != NULL)
    5268              :                 {
    5269              :                         /*
    5270              :                          * It's a grouping column, so add it to the input target as-is.
    5271              :                          */
    5272           12 :                         add_column_to_pathtarget(input_target, expr, sgref);
    5273              :                 }
    5274              :                 else
    5275              :                 {
    5276              :                         /*
    5277              :                          * Non-grouping column, so just remember the expression for later
    5278              :                          * call to pull_var_clause.
    5279              :                          */
    5280           26 :                         non_group_cols = lappend(non_group_cols, expr);
    5281              :                 }
    5282              : 
    5283           38 :                 i++;
    5284              :         }
    5285              : 
    5286              :         /*
    5287              :          * If there's a HAVING clause, we'll need the Vars it uses, too.
    5288              :          */
    5289           17 :         if (parse->havingQual)
    5290            1 :                 non_group_cols = lappend(non_group_cols, parse->havingQual);
    5291              : 
    5292              :         /*
    5293              :          * Pull out all the Vars mentioned in non-group cols (plus HAVING), and
    5294              :          * add them to the input target if not already present.  (A Var used
    5295              :          * directly as a GROUP BY item will be present already.)  Note this
    5296              :          * includes Vars used in resjunk items, so we are covering the needs of
    5297              :          * ORDER BY and window specifications.  Vars used within Aggrefs and
    5298              :          * WindowFuncs will be pulled out here, too.
    5299              :          */
    5300           17 :         non_group_vars = pull_var_clause((Node *) non_group_cols,
    5301              :                                                                          PVC_RECURSE_AGGREGATES |
    5302              :                                                                          PVC_RECURSE_WINDOWFUNCS |
    5303              :                                                                          PVC_INCLUDE_PLACEHOLDERS);
    5304           17 :         add_new_columns_to_pathtarget(input_target, non_group_vars);
    5305              : 
    5306              :         /* clean up cruft */
    5307           17 :         list_free(non_group_vars);
    5308           17 :         list_free(non_group_cols);
    5309              : 
    5310              :         /* XXX this causes some redundant cost calculation ... */
    5311           17 :         return set_pathtarget_cost_width(root, input_target);
    5312              : }
    5313              : 
    5314              : /*
    5315              :  * make_partial_grouping_target
    5316              :  *        Generate appropriate PathTarget for output of partial aggregate
    5317              :  *        (or partial grouping, if there are no aggregates) nodes.
    5318              :  *
    5319              :  * A partial aggregation node needs to emit all the same aggregates that
    5320              :  * a regular aggregation node would, plus any aggregates used in HAVING;
    5321              :  * except that the Aggref nodes should be marked as partial aggregates.
    5322              :  *
    5323              :  * In addition, we'd better emit any Vars and PlaceHolderVars that are
    5324              :  * used outside of Aggrefs in the aggregation tlist and HAVING.  (Presumably,
    5325              :  * these would be Vars that are grouped by or used in grouping expressions.)
    5326              :  *
    5327              :  * grouping_target is the tlist to be emitted by the topmost aggregation step.
    5328              :  * havingQual represents the HAVING clause.
    5329              :  */
    5330              : static PathTarget *
    5331            0 : make_partial_grouping_target(PlannerInfo *root,
    5332              :                                                          PathTarget *grouping_target,
    5333              :                                                          Node *havingQual)
    5334              : {

    5427              : /*
    5428              :  * mark_partial_aggref

    5496              : /*
    5497              :  * select_active_windows

    5500              :  */
    5501              : static List *
    5502            0 : select_active_windows(PlannerInfo *root, WindowFuncLists *wflists)
    5503              : {
    5504            0 :         List       *windowClause = root->parse->windowClause;
    5505            0 :         List       *result = NIL;
    5506              :         ListCell   *lc;
    5507            0 :         int                     nActive = 0;
    5508            0 :         WindowClauseSortData *actives = palloc(sizeof(WindowClauseSortData)
    5509            0 :                                                                                    * list_length(windowClause));
    5510              : 
    5511              :         /* First, construct an array of the active windows */
    5512            0 :         foreach(lc, windowClause)
    5513              :         {
    5514            0 :                 WindowClause *wc = lfirst_node(WindowClause, lc);
    5515              : 
    5516              :                 /* It's only active if wflists shows some related WindowFuncs */
    5517              :                 Assert(wc->winref <= wflists->maxWinRef);
    5518            0 :                 if (wflists->windowFuncs[wc->winref] == NIL)
    5519            0 :                         continue;
    5520              : 
    5521            0 :                 actives[nActive].wc = wc;       /* original clause */
    5522              : 
    5523              :                 /*
    5524              :                  * For sorting, we want the list of partition keys followed by the
    5525              :                  * list of sort keys. But pathkeys construction will remove duplicates
    5526              :                  * between the two, so we can as well (even though we can't detect all
    5527              :                  * of the duplicates, since some may come from ECs - that might mean
    5528              :                  * we miss optimization chances here). We must, however, ensure that
    5529              :                  * the order of entries is preserved with respect to the ones we do
    5530              :                  * keep.
    5531              :                  *
    5532              :                  * partitionClause and orderClause had their own duplicates removed in
    5533              :                  * parse analysis, so we're only concerned here with removing

    5535              :                  */

    5537            0 :                         list_concat_unique(list_copy(wc->partitionClause),
    5538            0 :                                                            wc->orderClause);

    5540              :         }
    5541              : 
    5542              :         /*
    5543              :          * Sort active windows by their partitioning/ordering clauses, ignoring
    5544              :          * any framing clauses, so that the windows that need the same sorting are
    5545              :          * adjacent in the list. When we come to generate paths, this will avoid
    5546              :          * inserting additional Sort nodes.
    5547              :          *
    5548              :          * This is how we implement a specific requirement from the SQL standard,
    5549              :          * which says that when two or more windows are order-equivalent (i.e.
    5550              :          * have matching partition and order clauses, even if their names or
    5551              :          * framing clauses differ), then all peer rows must be presented in the
    5552              :          * same order in all of them. If we allowed multiple sort nodes for such
    5553              :          * cases, we'd risk having the peer rows end up in different orders in
    5554              :          * equivalent windows due to sort instability. (See General Rule 4 of
    5555              :          * <window clause> in SQL2008 - SQL2016.)
    5556              :          *
    5557              :          * Additionally, if the entire list of clauses of one window is a prefix
    5558              :          * of another, put first the window with stronger sorting requirements.
    5559              :          * This way we will first sort for stronger window, and won't have to sort
    5560              :          * again for the weaker one.
    5561              :          */
    5562            0 :         qsort(actives, nActive, sizeof(WindowClauseSortData), common_prefix_cmp);

    5564              :         /* build ordered list of the original WindowClause nodes */
    5565            0 :         for (int i = 0; i < nActive; i++)

    5567              : 
    5568            0 :         pfree(actives);
    5569              : 
    5570            0 :         return result;
    5571              : }

    5573              : /*
    5574              :  * common_prefix_cmp
    5575              :  *        QSort comparison function for WindowClauseSortData
    5576              :  *
    5577              :  * Sort the windows by the required sorting clauses. First, compare the sort
    5578              :  * clauses themselves. Second, if one window's clauses are a prefix of another
    5579              :  * one's clauses, put the window with more sort clauses first.
    5580              :  */
    5581              : static int
    5582            0 : common_prefix_cmp(const void *a, const void *b)
    5583              : {
    5584            0 :         const WindowClauseSortData *wcsa = a;
    5585            0 :         const WindowClauseSortData *wcsb = b;
    5586              :         ListCell   *item_a;
    5587              :         ListCell   *item_b;
    5588              : 
    5589            0 :         forboth(item_a, wcsa->uniqueOrder, item_b, wcsb->uniqueOrder)
    5590              :         {
    5591            0 :                 SortGroupClause *sca = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, item_a);
    5592            0 :                 SortGroupClause *scb = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, item_b);
    5593              : 
    5594            0 :                 if (sca->tleSortGroupRef > scb->tleSortGroupRef)
    5595            0 :                         return -1;
    5596            0 :                 else if (sca->tleSortGroupRef < scb->tleSortGroupRef)
    5597            0 :                         return 1;
    5598            0 :                 else if (sca->sortop > scb->sortop)
    5599            0 :                         return -1;
    5600            0 :                 else if (sca->sortop < scb->sortop)
    5601            0 :                         return 1;
    5602            0 :                 else if (sca->nulls_first && !scb->nulls_first)
    5603            0 :                         return -1;
    5604            0 :                 else if (!sca->nulls_first && scb->nulls_first)
    5605            0 :                         return 1;
    5606              :                 /* no need to compare eqop, since it is fully determined by sortop */
    5607              :         }
    5608              : 
    5609            0 :         if (list_length(wcsa->uniqueOrder) > list_length(wcsb->uniqueOrder))
    5610            0 :                 return -1;
    5611            0 :         else if (list_length(wcsa->uniqueOrder) < list_length(wcsb->uniqueOrder))
    5612            0 :                 return 1;
    5613              : 
    5614            0 :         return 0;
    5615              : }

    5617              : /*
    5618              :  * make_window_input_target
    5619              :  *        Generate appropriate PathTarget for initial input to WindowAgg nodes.
    5620              :  *
    5621              :  * When the query has window functions, this function computes the desired
    5622              :  * target to be computed by the node just below the first WindowAgg.
    5623              :  * This tlist must contain all values needed to evaluate the window functions,
    5624              :  * compute the final target list, and perform any required final sort step.
    5625              :  * If multiple WindowAggs are needed, each intermediate one adds its window
    5626              :  * function results onto this base tlist; only the topmost WindowAgg computes
    5627              :  * the actual desired target list.
    5628              :  *
    5629              :  * This function is much like make_group_input_target, though not quite enough
    5630              :  * like it to share code.  As in that function, we flatten most expressions
    5631              :  * into their component variables.  But we do not want to flatten window
    5632              :  * PARTITION BY/ORDER BY clauses, since that might result in multiple
    5633              :  * evaluations of them, which would be bad (possibly even resulting in
    5634              :  * inconsistent answers, if they contain volatile functions).
    5635              :  * Also, we must not flatten GROUP BY clauses that were left unflattened by
    5636              :  * make_group_input_target, because we may no longer have access to the
    5637              :  * individual Vars in them.
    5638              :  *
    5639              :  * Another key difference from make_group_input_target is that we don't
    5640              :  * flatten Aggref expressions, since those are to be computed below the
    5641              :  * window functions and just referenced like Vars above that.
    5642              :  *
    5643              :  * 'final_target' is the query's final target list (in PathTarget form)
    5644              :  * 'activeWindows' is the list of active windows previously identified by
    5645              :  *                      select_active_windows.
    5646              :  *
    5647              :  * The result is the PathTarget to be computed by the plan node immediately
    5648              :  * below the first WindowAgg node.
    5649              :  */
    5650              : static PathTarget *
    5651            0 : make_window_input_target(PlannerInfo *root,
    5652              :                                                  PathTarget *final_target,
    5653              :                                                  List *activeWindows)
    5654              : {
    5655            0 :         Query      *parse = root->parse;
    5656              :         PathTarget *input_target;

    5658              :         List       *flattenable_cols;

    5660              :         int                     i;
    5661              :         ListCell   *lc;
    5662              : 
    5663              :         Assert(parse->hasWindowFuncs);
    5664              : 
    5665              :         /*
    5666              :          * Collect the sortgroupref numbers of window PARTITION/ORDER BY clauses

    5668              :          */
    5669            0 :         sgrefs = NULL;
    5670            0 :         foreach(lc, activeWindows)
    5671              :         {
    5672            0 :                 WindowClause *wc = lfirst_node(WindowClause, lc);
    5673              :                 ListCell   *lc2;
    5674              : 
    5675            0 :                 foreach(lc2, wc->partitionClause)
    5676              :                 {
    5677            0 :                         SortGroupClause *sortcl = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, lc2);
    5678              : 
    5679            0 :                         sgrefs = bms_add_member(sgrefs, sortcl->tleSortGroupRef);
    5680              :                 }
    5681            0 :                 foreach(lc2, wc->orderClause)
    5682              :                 {
    5683            0 :                         SortGroupClause *sortcl = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, lc2);
    5684              : 
    5685            0 :                         sgrefs = bms_add_member(sgrefs, sortcl->tleSortGroupRef);
    5686              :                 }
    5687              :         }
    5688              : 
    5689              :         /* Add in sortgroupref numbers of GROUP BY clauses, too */
    5690            0 :         foreach(lc, parse->groupClause)
    5691              :         {
    5692            0 :                 SortGroupClause *grpcl = lfirst_node(SortGroupClause, lc);
    5693              : 
    5694            0 :                 sgrefs = bms_add_member(sgrefs, grpcl->tleSortGroupRef);
    5695              :         }
    5696              : 
    5697              :         /*
    5698              :          * Construct a target containing all the non-flattenable targetlist items,

    5701            0 :         input_target = create_empty_pathtarget();
    5702            0 :         flattenable_cols = NIL;
    5703              : 
    5704            0 :         i = 0;
    5705            0 :         foreach(lc, final_target->exprs)
    5706              :         {
    5707            0 :                 Expr       *expr = (Expr *) lfirst(lc);
    5708            0 :                 Index           sgref = get_pathtarget_sortgroupref(final_target, i);
    5709              : 
    5710              :                 /*
    5711              :                  * Don't want to deconstruct window clauses or GROUP BY items.  (Note

    5715            0 :                 if (sgref != 0 && bms_is_member(sgref, sgrefs))

    5729            0 :                         flattenable_cols = lappend(flattenable_cols, expr);
    5730              :                 }
    5731              : 
    5732            0 :                 i++;
    5733              :         }
    5734              : 
    5735              :         /*
    5736              :          * Pull out all the Vars and Aggrefs mentioned in flattenable columns, and

    5750              : 
    5751              :         /* clean up cruft */
    5752            0 :         list_free(flattenable_vars);
    5753            0 :         list_free(flattenable_cols);
    5754              : 
    5755              :         /* XXX this causes some redundant cost calculation ... */
    5756            0 :         return set_pathtarget_cost_width(root, input_target);

    5758              : 
    5759              : /*
    5760              :  * make_pathkeys_for_window
    5761              :  *              Create a pathkeys list describing the required input ordering
    5762              :  *              for the given WindowClause.
    5763              :  *
    5764              :  * The required ordering is first the PARTITION keys, then the ORDER keys.
    5765              :  * In the future we might try to implement windowing using hashing, in which
    5766              :  * case the ordering could be relaxed, but for now we always sort.
    5767              :  */
    5768              : static List *
    5769            0 : make_pathkeys_for_window(PlannerInfo *root, WindowClause *wc,
    5770              :                                                  List *tlist)
    5771              : {
    5772              :         List       *window_pathkeys;
    5773              :         List       *window_sortclauses;
    5774              : 
    5775              :         /* Throw error if can't sort */
    5776            0 :         if (!grouping_is_sortable(wc->partitionClause))
    5777            0 :                 ereport(ERROR,
    5778              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
    5779              :                                  errmsg("could not implement window PARTITION BY"),
    5780              :                                  errdetail("Window partitioning columns must be of sortable datatypes.")));
    5781            0 :         if (!grouping_is_sortable(wc->orderClause))
    5782            0 :                 ereport(ERROR,
    5783              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
    5784              :                                  errmsg("could not implement window ORDER BY"),
    5785              :                                  errdetail("Window ordering columns must be of sortable datatypes.")));
    5786              : 
    5787              :         /* Okay, make the combined pathkeys */
    5788            0 :         window_sortclauses = list_concat_copy(wc->partitionClause, wc->orderClause);
    5789            0 :         window_pathkeys = make_pathkeys_for_sortclauses(root,
    5790              :                                                                                                         window_sortclauses,
    5791              :                                                                                                         tlist);
    5792            0 :         list_free(window_sortclauses);
    5793            0 :         return window_pathkeys;
    5794              : }
    5795              : 
    5796              : /*
    5797              :  * make_sort_input_target
    5798              :  *        Generate appropriate PathTarget for initial input to Sort step.
    5799              :  *
    5800              :  * If the query has ORDER BY, this function chooses the target to be computed
    5801              :  * by the node just below the Sort (and DISTINCT, if any, since Unique can't
    5802              :  * project) steps.  This might or might not be identical to the query's final
    5803              :  * output target.
    5804              :  *
    5805              :  * The main argument for keeping the sort-input tlist the same as the final
    5806              :  * is that we avoid a separate projection node (which will be needed if
    5807              :  * they're different, because Sort can't project).  However, there are also
    5808              :  * advantages to postponing tlist evaluation till after the Sort: it ensures
    5809              :  * a consistent order of evaluation for any volatile functions in the tlist,
    5810              :  * and if there's also a LIMIT, we can stop the query without ever computing
    5811              :  * tlist functions for later rows, which is beneficial for both volatile and
    5812              :  * expensive functions.
    5813              :  *
    5814              :  * Our current policy is to postpone volatile expressions till after the sort
    5815              :  * unconditionally (assuming that that's possible, ie they are in plain tlist
    5816              :  * columns and not ORDER BY/GROUP BY/DISTINCT columns).  We also prefer to
    5817              :  * postpone set-returning expressions, because running them beforehand would
    5818              :  * bloat the sort dataset, and because it might cause unexpected output order
    5819              :  * if the sort isn't stable.  However there's a constraint on that: all SRFs
    5820              :  * in the tlist should be evaluated at the same plan step, so that they can
    5821              :  * run in sync in nodeProjectSet.  So if any SRFs are in sort columns, we
    5822              :  * mustn't postpone any SRFs.  (Note that in principle that policy should
    5823              :  * probably get applied to the group/window input targetlists too, but we
    5824              :  * have not done that historically.)  Lastly, expensive expressions are
    5825              :  * postponed if there is a LIMIT, or if root->tuple_fraction shows that
    5826              :  * partial evaluation of the query is possible (if neither is true, we expect
    5827              :  * to have to evaluate the expressions for every row anyway), or if there are
    5828              :  * any volatile or set-returning expressions (since once we've put in a
    5829              :  * projection at all, it won't cost any more to postpone more stuff).
    5830              :  *
    5831              :  * Another issue that could potentially be considered here is that
    5832              :  * evaluating tlist expressions could result in data that's either wider
    5833              :  * or narrower than the input Vars, thus changing the volume of data that
    5834              :  * has to go through the Sort.  However, we usually have only a very bad
    5835              :  * idea of the output width of any expression more complex than a Var,
    5836              :  * so for now it seems too risky to try to optimize on that basis.
    5837              :  *
    5838              :  * Note that if we do produce a modified sort-input target, and then the
    5839              :  * query ends up not using an explicit Sort, no particular harm is done:
    5840              :  * we'll initially use the modified target for the preceding path nodes,
    5841              :  * but then change them to the final target with apply_projection_to_path.
    5842              :  * Moreover, in such a case the guarantees about evaluation order of
    5843              :  * volatile functions still hold, since the rows are sorted already.
    5844              :  *
    5845              :  * This function has some things in common with make_group_input_target and
    5846              :  * make_window_input_target, though the detailed rules for what to do are
    5847              :  * different.  We never flatten/postpone any grouping or ordering columns;
    5848              :  * those are needed before the sort.  If we do flatten a particular
    5849              :  * expression, we leave Aggref and WindowFunc nodes alone, since those were
    5850              :  * computed earlier.
    5851              :  *
    5852              :  * 'final_target' is the query's final target list (in PathTarget form)
    5853              :  * 'have_postponed_srfs' is an output argument, see below
    5854              :  *
    5855              :  * The result is the PathTarget to be computed by the plan node immediately
    5856              :  * below the Sort step (and the Distinct step, if any).  This will be
    5857              :  * exactly final_target if we decide a projection step wouldn't be helpful.
    5858              :  *
    5859              :  * In addition, *have_postponed_srfs is set to true if we choose to postpone
    5860              :  * any set-returning functions to after the Sort.
    5861              :  */
    5862              : static PathTarget *
    5863           17 : make_sort_input_target(PlannerInfo *root,

    5865              :                                            bool *have_postponed_srfs)

    5871              :         bool       *postpone_col;
    5872              :         bool            have_srf;
    5873              :         bool            have_volatile;
    5874              :         bool            have_expensive;
    5875              :         bool            have_srf_sortcols;
    5876              :         bool            postpone_srfs;
    5877              :         List       *postponable_cols;
    5878              :         List       *postponable_vars;
    5879              :         int                     i;
    5880              :         ListCell   *lc;
    5881              : 
    5882              :         /* Shouldn't get here unless query has ORDER BY */

    5884              : 
    5885           17 :         *have_postponed_srfs = false;   /* default result */
    5886              :

    5893           17 :         i = 0;

    6066              :  * top level of the targetlist of a ProjectSet plan node.  If we have any SRFs
    6067              :  * that are not at top level, we need to split up the evaluation into multiple
    6068              :  * plan levels in which each level satisfies this constraint.  This function
    6069              :  * modifies each Path of an upperrel that (might) compute any SRFs in its

    6164              : /*
    6165              :  * expression_planner
    6166              :  *              Perform planner's transformations on a standalone expression.
    6167              :  *
    6168              :  * Various utility commands need to evaluate expressions that are not part
    6169              :  * of a plannable query.  They can do so using the executor's regular
    6170              :  * expression-execution machinery, but first the expression has to be fed
    6171              :  * through here to transform it from parser output to something executable.
    6172              :  *
    6173              :  * Currently, we disallow sublinks in standalone expressions, so there's no
    6174              :  * real "planning" involved here.  (That might not always be true though.)
    6175              :  * What we must do is run eval_const_expressions to ensure that any function
    6176              :  * calls are converted to positional notation and function default arguments
    6177              :  * get inserted.  The fact that constant subexpressions get simplified is a
    6178              :  * side-effect that is useful when the expression will get evaluated more than
    6179              :  * once.  Also, we must fix operator function IDs.
    6180              :  *
    6181              :  * This does not return any information about dependencies of the expression.
    6182              :  * Hence callers should use the results only for the duration of the current
    6183              :  * query.  Callers that would like to cache the results for longer should use
    6184              :  * expression_planner_with_deps, probably via the plancache.
    6185              :  *
    6186              :  * Note: this must not make any damaging changes to the passed-in expression
    6187              :  * tree.  (It would actually be okay to apply fix_opfuncids to it, but since
    6188              :  * we first do an expression_tree_mutator-based walk, what is returned will
    6189              :  * be a new node tree.)  The result is constructed in the current memory
    6190              :  * context; beware that this can leak a lot of additional stuff there, too.
    6191              :  */
    6192              : Expr *
    6193          332 : expression_planner(Expr *expr)
    6194              : {
    6195              :         Node       *result;
    6196              : 
    6197              :         /*
    6198              :          * Convert named-argument function calls, insert default arguments and
    6199              :          * simplify constant subexprs
    6200              :          */
    6201          332 :         result = eval_const_expressions(NULL, (Node *) expr);
    6202              : 
    6203              :         /* Fill in opfuncid values if missing */
    6204          332 :         fix_opfuncids(result);
    6205              : 
    6206          332 :         return (Expr *) result;
    6207              : }
    6208              : 
    6209              : /*
    6210              :  * expression_planner_with_deps
    6211              :  *              Perform planner's transformations on a standalone expression,
    6212              :  *              returning expression dependency information along with the result.
    6213              :  *
    6214              :  * This is identical to expression_planner() except that it also returns
    6215              :  * information about possible dependencies of the expression, ie identities of
    6216              :  * objects whose definitions affect the result.  As in a PlannedStmt, these
    6217              :  * are expressed as a list of relation Oids and a list of PlanInvalItems.
    6218              :  */
    6219              : Expr *
    6220            0 : expression_planner_with_deps(Expr *expr,

    6225              :         PlannerGlobal glob;
    6226              :         PlannerInfo root;
    6227              : 
    6228              :         /* Make up dummy planner state so we can use setrefs machinery */
    6229            0 :         MemSet(&glob, 0, sizeof(glob));

    6231            0 :         glob.relationOids = NIL;
    6232            0 :         glob.invalItems = NIL;
    6233              : 
    6234            0 :         MemSet(&root, 0, sizeof(root));
    6235            0 :         root.type = T_PlannerInfo;
    6236            0 :         root.glob = &glob;
    6237              : 
    6238              :         /*
    6239              :          * Convert named-argument function calls, insert default arguments and

    6242              :          */

    6244              : 
    6245              :         /* Fill in opfuncid values if missing */
    6246            0 :         fix_opfuncids(result);

    6248              :         /*
    6249              :          * Now walk the finished expression to find anything else we ought to

    6251              :          */
    6252            0 :         (void) extract_query_dependencies_walker(result, &root);
    6253              : 
    6254            0 :         *relationOids = glob.relationOids;
    6255            0 :         *invalItems = glob.invalItems;

    6260              : 
    6261              : /*
    6262              :  * plan_cluster_use_sort
    6263              :  *              Use the planner to decide how CLUSTER should implement sorting
    6264              :  *

    6266              :  * (which is already known to be a btree index).  Decide whether it's
    6267              :  * cheaper to do an indexscan or a seqscan-plus-sort to execute the CLUSTER.
    6268              :  * Return true to use sorting, false to use an indexscan.
    6269              :  *
    6270              :  * Note: caller had better already hold some type of lock on the table.
    6271              :  */

    6273            0 : plan_cluster_use_sort(Oid tableOid, Oid indexOid)

    6282              :         Cost            comparisonCost;
    6283              :         Path       *seqScanPath;
    6284              :         Path            seqScanAndSortPath;
    6285              :         IndexPath  *indexScanPath;
    6286              :         ListCell   *lc;
    6287              : 
    6288              :         /* We can short-circuit the cost comparison if indexscans are disabled */

    6291              : 
    6292              :         /* Set up mostly-dummy planner state */

    6374              : /*
    6375              :  * plan_create_index_workers
    6376              :  *              Use the planner to decide how many parallel worker processes
    6377              :  *              CREATE INDEX should request for use
    6378              :  *
    6379              :  * tableOid is the table on which the index is to be built.  indexOid is the
    6380              :  * OID of an index to be created or reindexed (which must be a btree index).
    6381              :  *
    6382              :  * Return value is the number of parallel worker processes to request.  It
    6383              :  * may be unsafe to proceed if this is 0.  Note that this does not include the
    6384              :  * leader participating as a worker (value is always a number of parallel
    6385              :  * worker processes).
    6386              :  *
    6387              :  * Note: caller had better already hold some type of lock on the table and
    6388              :  * index.
    6389              :  */
    6390              : int
    6391          114 : plan_create_index_workers(Oid tableOid, Oid indexOid)
    6392              : {
    6393              :         PlannerInfo *root;
    6394              :         Query      *query;
    6395              :         PlannerGlobal *glob;
    6396              :         RangeTblEntry *rte;
    6397              :         Relation        heap;
    6398              :         Relation        index;
    6399              :         RelOptInfo *rel;
    6400              :         int                     parallel_workers;
    6401              :         BlockNumber heap_blocks;
    6402              :         double          reltuples;
    6403              :         double          allvisfrac;
    6404              : 
    6405              :         /*
    6406              :          * We don't allow performing parallel operation in standalone backend or
    6407              :          * when parallelism is disabled.
    6408              :          */
    6409          114 :         if (!IsUnderPostmaster || max_parallel_maintenance_workers == 0)
    6410            4 :                 return 0;
    6411              : 
    6412              :         /* Set up largely-dummy planner state */
    6413          110 :         query = makeNode(Query);
    6414          110 :         query->commandType = CMD_SELECT;
    6415              : 
    6416          110 :         glob = makeNode(PlannerGlobal);
    6417              : 
    6418          110 :         root = makeNode(PlannerInfo);
    6419          110 :         root->parse = query;
    6420          110 :         root->glob = glob;
    6421          110 :         root->query_level = 1;
    6422          110 :         root->planner_cxt = CurrentMemoryContext;
    6423          110 :         root->wt_param_id = -1;
    6424              : 
    6425              :         /*
    6426              :          * Build a minimal RTE.
    6427              :          *
    6428              :          * Mark the RTE with inh = true.  This is a kludge to prevent
    6429              :          * get_relation_info() from fetching index info, which is necessary
    6430              :          * because it does not expect that any IndexOptInfo is currently
    6431              :          * undergoing REINDEX.
    6432              :          */
    6433          110 :         rte = makeNode(RangeTblEntry);
    6434          110 :         rte->rtekind = RTE_RELATION;
    6435          110 :         rte->relid = tableOid;
    6436          110 :         rte->relkind = RELKIND_RELATION;     /* Don't be too picky. */
    6437          110 :         rte->rellockmode = AccessShareLock;
    6438          110 :         rte->lateral = false;
    6439          110 :         rte->inh = true;
    6440          110 :         rte->inFromCl = true;
    6441          110 :         query->rtable = list_make1(rte);
    6442              : 
    6443              :         /* Set up RTE/RelOptInfo arrays */
    6444          110 :         setup_simple_rel_arrays(root);
    6445              : 
    6446              :         /* Build RelOptInfo */
    6447          110 :         rel = build_simple_rel(root, 1, NULL);
    6448              : 
    6449              :         /* Rels are assumed already locked by the caller */
    6450          110 :         heap = table_open(tableOid, NoLock);
    6451          110 :         index = index_open(indexOid, NoLock);
    6452              : 
    6453              :         /*
    6454              :          * Determine if it's safe to proceed.
    6455              :          *
    6456              :          * Currently, parallel workers can't access the leader's temporary tables.
    6457              :          * Furthermore, any index predicate or index expressions must be parallel
    6458              :          * safe.
    6459              :          */
    6460          110 :         if (heap->rd_rel->relpersistence == RELPERSISTENCE_TEMP ||
    6461          108 :                 !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) RelationGetIndexExpressions(index)) ||
    6462          108 :                 !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) RelationGetIndexPredicate(index)))
    6463              :         {
    6464            2 :                 parallel_workers = 0;
    6465            2 :                 goto done;
    6466              :         }
    6467              : 
    6468              :         /*
    6469              :          * If parallel_workers storage parameter is set for the table, accept that
    6470              :          * as the number of parallel worker processes to launch (though still cap
    6471              :          * at max_parallel_maintenance_workers).  Note that we deliberately do not
    6472              :          * consider any other factor when parallel_workers is set. (e.g., memory
    6473              :          * use by workers.)
    6474              :          */
    6475          108 :         if (rel->rel_parallel_workers != -1)
    6476              :         {
    6477            0 :                 parallel_workers = Min(rel->rel_parallel_workers,
    6478              :                                                            max_parallel_maintenance_workers);
    6479            0 :                 goto done;
    6480              :         }
    6481              : 
    6482              :         /*
    6483              :          * Estimate heap relation size ourselves, since rel->pages cannot be
    6484              :          * trusted (heap RTE was marked as inheritance parent)

    6486          108 :         estimate_rel_size(heap, NULL, &heap_blocks, &reltuples, &allvisfrac);
    6487              : 
    6488              :         /*
    6489              :          * Determine number of workers to scan the heap relation using generic
    6490              :          * model
    6491              :          */
    6492          108 :         parallel_workers = compute_parallel_worker(rel, heap_blocks, -1,
    6493              :                                                                                            max_parallel_maintenance_workers);
    6494              : 
    6495              :         /*
    6496              :          * Cap workers based on available maintenance_work_mem as needed.
    6497              :          *
    6498              :          * Note that each tuplesort participant receives an even share of the
    6499              :          * total maintenance_work_mem budget.  Aim to leave participants
    6500              :          * (including the leader as a participant) with no less than 32MB of
    6501              :          * memory.  This leaves cases where maintenance_work_mem is set to 64MB
    6502              :          * immediately past the threshold of being capable of launching a single
    6503              :          * parallel worker to sort.
    6504              :          */
    6505          108 :         while (parallel_workers > 0 &&
    6506            0 :                    maintenance_work_mem / (parallel_workers + 1) < 32768L)
    6507            0 :                 parallel_workers--;
    6508              : 
    6509          108 : done:
    6510          110 :         index_close(index, NoLock);
    6511          110 :         table_close(heap, NoLock);
    6512              : 
    6513          110 :         return parallel_workers;
    6514              : }

    6851              : /*

    6856              :  * lists of paths here, so the caller can add additional partial or non-partial
    6857              :  * paths and must afterward call gather_grouping_paths and set_cheapest on

    7247              : /*
    7248              :  * Generate Gather and Gather Merge paths for a grouping relation or partial
    7249              :  * grouping relation.
    7250              :  *
    7251              :  * generate_gather_paths does most of the work, but we also consider a special
    7252              :  * case: we could try sorting the data by the group_pathkeys and then applying
    7253              :  * Gather Merge.
    7254              :  *
    7255              :  * NB: This function shouldn't be used for anything other than a grouped or
    7256              :  * partially grouped relation not only because of the fact that it explicitly
    7257              :  * references group_pathkeys but we pass "true" as the third argument to
    7258              :  * generate_gather_paths().
    7259              :  */

    7261            0 : gather_grouping_paths(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
    7262              : {
    7263              :         ListCell   *lc;

    7266              :         /* Try Gather for unordered paths and Gather Merge for ordered ones. */

    7268              : 
    7269              :         /* Try cheapest partial path + explicit Sort + Gather Merge. */
    7270            0 :         cheapest_partial_path = linitial(rel->partial_pathlist);
    7271            0 :         if (!pathkeys_contained_in(root->group_pathkeys,
    7272              :                                                            cheapest_partial_path->pathkeys))
    7273              :         {
    7274              :                 Path       *path;
    7275              :                 double          total_groups;
    7276              :

    7342              : /*

    7369            3 :                 return false;
    7370              :         }
    7371              :

    7376              : /*
    7377              :  * apply_scanjoin_target_to_paths
    7378              :  *
    7379              :  * Adjust the final scan/join relation, and recursively all of its children,

    7381              :  * this as a separate planning step with a new RelOptInfo at the toplevel and
    7382              :  * for each child relation, but doing it this way is noticeably cheaper.
    7383              :  * Maybe that problem can be solved at some point, but for now we do this.
    7384              :  *
    7385              :  * If tlist_same_exprs is true, then the scan/join target to be applied has
    7386              :  * the same expressions as the existing reltarget, so we need only insert the
    7387              :  * appropriate sortgroupref information.  By avoiding the creation of
    7388              :  * projection paths we save effort both immediately and at plan creation time.
    7389              :  */

    7402              :         /* This recurses, so be paranoid. */
    7403          491 :         check_stack_depth();
    7404              : 
    7405              :         /*
    7406              :          * If the rel is partitioned, we want to drop its existing paths and
    7407              :          * generate new ones.  This function would still be correct if we kept the
    7408              :          * existing paths: we'd modify them to generate the correct target above
    7409              :          * the partitioning Append, and then they'd compete on cost with paths
    7410              :          * generating the target below the Append.  However, in our current cost
    7411              :          * model the latter way is always the same or cheaper cost, so modifying
    7412              :          * the existing paths would just be useless work.  Moreover, when the cost
    7413              :          * is the same, varying roundoff errors might sometimes allow an existing
    7414              :          * path to be picked, resulting in undesirable cross-platform plan
    7415              :          * variations.  So we drop old paths and thereby force the work to be done
    7416              :          * below the Append, except in the case of a non-parallel-safe target.
    7417              :          *
    7418              :          * Some care is needed, because we have to allow generate_gather_paths to
    7419              :          * see the old partial paths in the next stanza.  Hence, zap the main
    7420              :          * pathlist here, then allow generate_gather_paths to add path(s) to the
    7421              :          * main list, and finally zap the partial pathlist.

    7605              : /*
    7606              :  * create_partitionwise_grouping_paths
    7607              :  *
    7608              :  * If the partition keys of input relation are part of the GROUP BY clause, all
    7609              :  * the rows belonging to a given group come from a single partition.  This
    7610              :  * allows aggregation/grouping over a partitioned relation to be broken down
    7611              :  * into aggregation/grouping on each partition.  This should be no worse, and
    7612              :  * often better, than the normal approach.
    7613              :  *
    7614              :  * However, if the GROUP BY clause does not contain all the partition keys,
    7615              :  * rows from a given group may be spread across multiple partitions. In that
    7616              :  * case, we perform partial aggregation for each group, append the results,
    7617              :  * and then finalize aggregation.  This is less certain to win than the
    7618              :  * previous case.  It may win if the PartialAggregate stage greatly reduces

    7756              : /*
    7757              :  * group_by_has_partkey
    7758              :  *

    7763            0 : group_by_has_partkey(RelOptInfo *input_rel,
